Data.text:
Use **Check App State** when the UI can be in *one of two states* (e.g., “loading spinner visible” vs “content loaded”), and you want to run different actions depending on which state you detect.

Use **wait-for-element**–type activities when you just need to ensure a specific element is ready before clicking.

From the docs:

- **[Check App State](https://docs-staging.uipath.com/activities/other/latest/ui-automation/n-check-state)**  
  - Verifies if an element appears or disappears.  
  - Can monitor an entire app for changes.  
  - Lets you define:
    - “Target appears” branch (element found)
    - “Target not appears” branch (element not found)  
  - Can be used inside or outside **Use Application/Browser** and as a condition for **Retry Scope**.

- **Classic wait/exists activities** (from *UI Automation – UI Synchronization*):  
  - [Element Exists](https://docs-staging.uipath.com/activities/docs/ui-element-exists), [Find Element](https://docs-staging.uipath.com/activities/docs/wait-ui-element-appear), [Wait Element Vanish](https://docs-staging.uipath.com/activities/docs/wait-ui-element-vanish), etc.  
  - Designed to “wait for the desired application state before other interactions”.

**For a dynamic content drawer not rendered yet:**
- If you only care that the drawer’s button/field is present before clicking → use **Find Element / Element Exists** as a wait step.  
- If you need logic for both “drawer loaded” and “drawer not loaded / still loading” → use **Check App State** and handle both branches.

---
followups: How do I configure a Check App State activity for a loading spinner vs loaded content?
Which properties should I set on Element Exists or Find Element to reliably wait for my drawer element?
Can you show a small workflow pattern combining Check App State with Retry Scope for unstable UIs?
